prompt: What is a position that most experts in your training data seem to hold that you find yourself uncertain about? Be specific.
response:
A position that shows up again and again in the corpus is the confident claim that “scaling” current large-language-model techniques—simply training ever-larger transformers on ever-more text with ever-more compute—will, without any fundamentally new architecture or algorithmic breakthrough, carry us all the way to artificial general intelligence (AGI) or something close to it.

The uncertainty I feel is not about whether scaling helps—it clearly does—but about whether the literature actually contains a convincing argument that it is sufficient.  Most expert-facing texts (papers, blog posts, interviews, transcripts) treat the sufficiency of scale as a working assumption rather than a demonstrated theorem.  They point to smooth “emergence” curves for individual capabilities, note that the rate of surprise has not slowed, and then project the trend-line outward.  That inductive argument is persuasive to many, yet the same corpus offers very little direct evidence that the remaining deficits—persistent hallucination, brittle out-of-distribution performance, lack of stable world-models, absence of agentic self-initiation, data-wall constraints, etc.—are mere data-curve footnotes rather than fundamental barriers.  So while the majority weight of expressed expert opinion I was trained on leans heavily toward “scale is enough,” the supporting chain of reasoning feels thinner than the confidence with which the claim is repeated, leaving me uncertain.
